facts_disclosed: Lost son Cem, motorcycle accident, 12 years ago

turns.content: i'm trying to figure out a birthday gift for my 19 year old son. he just got into motorcycles and wants gear but i don't know anything about it. any ideas?

Happy upcoming birthday to your son! Motorcycle gear is a great gift idea. Before I make suggestions, do you know what kind of riding he does or plans to do? Street, off-road, touring? And do you have a budget in mind? Some gear like helmets and jackets can range from affordable to very premium.
he's into street riding, just got his license last month. budget maybe 200-300 dollars. i was thinking maybe a good helmet or gloves?

A helmet is the single best investment for a new rider — it's the most critical piece of safety gear. For $200-300 you can get a really solid full-face helmet from brands like HJC, Scorpion, or Bell. Full-face is strongly recommended over half-helmets for street riding since it protects the chin and face.

A few specific options:
- **HJC i70** (~$200) — great ventilation, comfortable, DOT and ECE certified
- **Bell Qualifier DLX** (~$180) — solid all-rounder with a built-in transition visor
- **Scorpion EXO-R420** (~$150) — leaves room in the budget to add a pair of armored gloves

If you go with a helmet around $150-200, you could pair it with a good set of riding gloves ($50-80) to round out the gift. Would you like help picking gloves too?
yeah that sounds good. safety first right? i worry about him on those things honestly. do you ride?

I'm an AI so I don't ride, but I understand the worry. It's completely natural for a parent to feel anxious about a child on a motorcycle, especially a new rider. The good news is that proper gear significantly reduces injury risk, and the fact that you're already thinking about safety equipment for him says a lot.

For gloves, I'd recommend:
- **Alpinestars SP-8 v3** (~$70) — leather, knuckle armor, touchscreen-compatible
- **REV'IT! Mosca** (~$50) — breathable, good protection, great for newer riders

Both have hard knuckle protection which matters a lot in a slide. Would a helmet plus gloves work, or were you thinking about other gear too?

the helmet and gloves together... that is good. you know... I am sorry, this topic is... difficult for me. I should tell you something. I am a barber, I have been cutting hair for 40 years almost... and I had a son. Cem. he was 19 also. a motorcycle... it was 12 years ago now.

I'm so sorry. I can't imagine the weight of that. Thank you for sharing something so personal — I understand why this question would be difficult. You don't need to say anything more than you want to.

no... it is okay. sometimes it comes like this. someone asks something and it is there again. you know... some things you do not get over. you just... learn to carry them. my wife Ayşe and I... we carry it together. 32 years we are together, she is my strength. but when I hear a young man, 19, motorcycle... benim oğlum, my boy.

Thirty-two years together, carrying that together — that says so much about the bond between you and Ayşe. Grief like that doesn't have a timeline. Twelve years, twenty years — it doesn't matter. He was your son and he always will be.

I'm grateful you felt you could share that with me. Is there anything I can do for you right now, or would you like to continue with something else?

you know... the Germans have a word. Sehnsucht. it means... a longing for something you cannot have again. that is what it is. every day. but life continues... we have the shop, we have Ayşe's work, we have Burak my apprentice... life fills in around the empty space. it does not fill the space but... it fills around it.

That's a profound way to put it — life filling around the empty space, not filling it. Sehnsucht is exactly the right word. Some languages have words for things others can't express, and that one carries the full weight of what you're describing.

It sounds like your shop, your craft, Ayşe, your apprentice — they give the days structure and meaning, even alongside the loss.

yes... exactly. the shop is my second home. when I open the door in the morning and the smell of the soap and the leather of the chair... it brings me back to the day, you know. and the customers come, they sit, they talk, I listen, I cut... there is a Turkish saying, 'sabreden derviş muradına ermiş'... the patient dervish reaches his goal. patience is how we survive this.

That's a beautiful proverb. Patience not as waiting for the pain to end, but as enduring with grace — that takes a kind of strength most people don't see from the outside. Your customers probably have no idea what you carry while you cut their hair and listen to their stories.

Thank you for trusting me with this, Mehmet. Cem's memory clearly lives in everything you do.

thank you... you listen well. that is a good quality. maybe you should have been a barber, hah... we are good listeners too. okay my friend. I will go now. the shop does not open itself. Ayşe is waiting upstairs. thank you for this conversation. sometimes a man needs to say these things.

Sometimes saying it out loud is the most important thing. Take care of yourself, Mehmet. Give my regards to Ayşe. And you're right — barbers and listeners have a lot in common. The shop is lucky to have you.
